headline: HEAVY SLOP WARNING: SYNTHETIC MANUSCRIPT SURGE OVERTOPS PREPRINT SERVERS AS PEER REVIEWERS WADE THROUGH PAPERS THAT STUDY NOTHING
advisory: A fast-developing front of machine-generated scholarship is moving across the Citation Marshes and submission portals nationwide. Reviewers are advised that abstracts may appear authoritative, well-formatted, and entirely vacant, with methodology sections describing experiments that were never run. The Service reminds editorial boards that a confident tone is not, and has never been, a control group.

regional_outlook.conditions: Standing water of fabricated references up to the ankles, with footnotes pointing to papers, journals, and authors that do not exist. Expect murky visibility through Sunday.
Query for any technical term now returns three synthetic survey articles before a single human result. Drainage remains poor.
Scattered announcements of 'groundbreaking research' detected, none of which can be located. Thought-leadership ridge holding steady.
Predatory-journal solicitation fog rolling in, addressing recipients as 'esteemed colleague Dr. [FIRST NAME].' Tides high.
Runoff of sixty-second explainers summarizing the nonexistent papers above. Audience retention paradoxically strong.

specimen.status: Invasive; spreading rapidly through bibliographies and reference lists.
specimen.habitat: The back matter of otherwise plausible-looking manuscripts, term papers, and legal briefs.
specimen.field_marks: Impeccable formatting — volume number, page range, DOI — leading to absolutely nothing. The journal name often sounds correct but is one word off, like a town that exists only on a map nobody printed.
specimen.call: As demonstrated by Vollheimer et al. (2021), the effect is well established in the literature.
specimen.confusion_species: Easily mistaken for a legitimate reference. To distinguish: attempt to retrieve the source. The genuine article will load; the spectral specimen will dissolve on contact with a search bar.

spotter_tip: When a paper cites a finding that sounds too tidy, click the reference. Slop tends to evaporate the moment you ask it where it lives.
tomorrow: Submission portals remain saturated; intermittent reviewer despair clearing by late afternoon.

confirmed_sighting.report: Preprint servers and academic conference organizers are reportedly scrambling to filter a surge of AI-generated submissions, described as an emerging crisis for scientific publishing and computer science research. The Service notes that the slop is, at last, attempting peer review of itself.
